segments.inferno-30-g51: E poi che functions as a compound conjunction meaning “and after” (poi che = after the fact). "i due rabbiosi fuor passati" uses the archaic/poetic fuor (cf. fuori/furono) with the participle passati to mean “had passed” (a perfect sense); "i due rabbiosi" is the subject (the two furious ones). "sovra cu’ io avea l’occhio tenuto" is a subordinate phrase: sovra + cu’ (archaic form of cui = whom) introduces the relative/relative-like complement, and avea + tenuto is an imperfect + past participle (avea tenuto = I had kept) expressing that his eye had been fixed on them.
segments.inferno-30-g52: rivolsilo is a past (perfect/simple-past) form with an enclitic object lo: rivolsi + lo = “I turned it back/turned it again.” The infinitive a guardar gives purpose/result (to look at), and li altri mal nati is the direct object phrase meaning “the other ill-born ones” (li = the, mal nati = ill/evil-born).
segments.inferno-30-g53: Io vidi un is a simple main clause in passato remoto: vidi = I saw; un = one/a man (indefinite). The short clause introduces the figure Dante perceives.
segments.inferno-30-g54: fatto a guisa di lëuto is a past participle phrase describing that figure: fatto (made/formed) + a guisa di (in the manner/likeness of) + lëuto (archaic spelling for liuto = lute). The whole is an adjectival phrase modifying the preceding un.
segments.inferno-30-g55: pur ch’ introduces a concessive subordinate clause (‘although/even though’) and requires the subjunctival/pluperfect form avesse avuta (che + elli avesse avuta = that he had had). Avessa avuta is a compound pluperfect subjunctive (avere in subjunctive + past participle avuta) meaning “had had.” l’anguinaia tronca is the direct object (the groin/truncated groin), and da l’altro che l’uomo ha forcuto is an agent/ablative phrase with a relative clause (the other which man has forcuto).
segments.inferno-30-g56: La grave idropesì is a noun phrase: La (definite article) + grave (adjective, “severe/serious”) + idropesì (archaic form of idropesia/dropsy). It names the disease that will be described in the following relative clause.
segments.inferno-30-g57: This is a relative clause starting with che (that/which). sì is an intensifier (“so/so much”), dispaia is a present-form verb meaning here “wastes/disfigures” or “throws out of proportion,” and takes le membra (the limbs) as its object. con l’omor (with the humour/ bodily fluid) introduces the means, and che mal converte is a further relative clause modifying omor: che (which) + mal (badly) + converte (turns/transforms).
segments.inferno-30-g58: Another relative/result clause: che (so/so much that) + ’l viso (the face) + non risponde (does not correspond/respond) + a la ventraia (to the belly/entrails). risponde here means “correspond/agree with” (the face does not match the swollen belly).
segments.inferno-30-g59: faceva + accusative + infinitive is a causative construction: faceva (imperfect of fare = made/caused) + lui (him) + tener (to keep) + le labbra aperte (the lips open). come l’etico fa is a simile: come = as/like, l’etico (the consumptive/consumption-stricken person) + fa (does).
segments.inferno-30-g60: This closes the simile with a relative clause: che (who/which) + per la sete (because of thirst) gives the reason, and l’un verso ’l mento e l’altro in sù rinverte describes the action: l’un (one lip) verso ’l mento (toward the chin) e l’altro (and the other) in sù (upward) rinverte (turns back/turns up). rinverte is a present tense verb ‘turns back/turns upward.’
segments.inferno-30-g61: "O voi che sanz’ alcuna pena siete," is a vocative address: O voi (oh, you) + a relative clause che ... siete (who are). Sanz' is the poetic contraction of senza ('without'), and alcuna pena = 'any pain/suffering' (alcuna here = 'any'). The clause literally: 'O you who are without any suffering.'
segments.inferno-30-g62: e non so io perché, = 'and I do not know why'. Non negates the verb; so is present indicative of sapere; io is an emphatic subject pronoun placed after the verb for emphasis; perché is interrogative 'why'.
segments.inferno-30-g63: nel mondo gramo = 'in the wretched world'. Nel = in + the; mondo here refers to earthly life/the world; gramo is an older adjective meaning 'wretched' or 'miserable'.
segments.inferno-30-g64: diss' elli a noi, = 'he said to us' in a poetically contracted form: diss' stands for disse (passato remoto 'said'); elli is an archaic third-person pronoun 'he'; a noi = 'to us'. The apostrophe marks Dante's metrical elision.
segments.inferno-30-g65: «guardate e attendete a la miseria del maestro Adamo;" Imperative plural guardate (look/watch) and attendete (pay heed) addressed to 'voi' from above. a la miseria = 'to the misery' (i.e. 'pay attention to the misery'); maestro Adamo = 'Master Adam' (maestro as title).
segments.inferno-30-g66: io ebbi, = 'I had,' with io emphatic subject before the verb; ebbi is the passato remoto of avere ('I had'). In context it begins a temporal/contrastive clause ('I had while alive').
segments.inferno-30-g67: vivo, = postpositive adjective 'alive' (literally 'I had, alive,' = 'while I was alive'); placed after ebbi to mark the temporal sense ('when I was alive').
segments.inferno-30-g68: assai di quel ch’i’ volli, = 'much of that which I wanted.' Assai = 'much' or 'a great deal'; di quel che = 'of that which' (quel + relative ch' = che); i' is the poetic clitic for io (here representing the subject of volli); volli is passato remoto of volere ('I wanted'/'I wished').
segments.inferno-30-g69: e ora, = simple connective 'and now,' introducing the lament that follows; ora = 'now' (temporal).
segments.inferno-30-g70: lasso! = an exclamation meaning 'alas!' or 'woe!'—an emotional interjection expressing weariness or lament.
segments.inferno-30-g106: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' introducing the clause. L’un di lor is a compressed, poetic form meaning 'the one of them' (l’ = il + elision; un = uno), i.e. 'one of them' as the subject of the following relative clause and action.
segments.inferno-30-g107: Che is the relative pronoun 'who/that' referring back to 'l’uno di lor'. Si recò is reflexive recarsi in the passato remoto (recò) used idiomatically here to mean 'was moved/turned' or 'became' (to a state), followed by a prepositional phrase a noia 'to annoyance'. The phrase forse d’esser nomato sì oscuro is an explanatory infinitive clause (di+essere contracted to d'esser) meaning 'perhaps at being called so obscure', with nomato as a past participle used adjectivally and sì modifying oscuro.
segments.inferno-30-g108: Col pugno is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with the fist'. Li is a clitic pronoun placed before the verb (here functioning as a dative 'to him' or 'at him'), and percosse is the passato remoto (3rd sg) of percuotere/percosse 'struck'. L’epa croia is the noun phrase 'the coarse paunch' (l’ = il + elision; epa = paunch; croia = coarse/rough) and is the direct object struck; the word order (clitic + verb + overt noun) is normal in older/poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-30-g109: Quella sonò come fosse un tamburo; = 'That sounded as if it were a drum.' Sonò is passato remoto of sonare ('sounded'/'rang'); come + fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) forms a simile 'as if it were.' Quella refers back to the sound.
segments.inferno-30-g110: e mastro Adamo li percosse il volto col braccio suo, = 'and Master Adam struck his face with his arm.' Percosse is passato remoto of percotere/percuotere ('struck'); col = con + il; braccio suo places the possessive after the noun, 'his arm.' The clitic li here is part of the object phrase ('struck him/them/the face of him') as used in the poetry.
segments.inferno-30-g111: che non parve men duro, = relative clause modifying 'braccio': 'which did not seem any less hard.' Parve is passato remoto of parere ('seemed'); men is an archaic form of meno ('less').
segments.inferno-30-g112: dicendo a lui: = present participle dicendo ('saying') introduces the direct speech that follows; a lui = 'to him.' The participle attaches to the main clause action (he struck ... saying to him).
segments.inferno-30-g113: «Ancor che mi sia tolto lo muover per le membra che son gravi, = 'Although the ability to move has been taken from me because my limbs are heavy.' Ancor che introduces a concessive clause requiring the subjunctive (sia tolto: present subjunctive of essere + past participle tolto = 'has been taken'); lo muover is a nominalized infinitive 'the moving/motion' (object); per le membra che son gravi indicates cause/means ('because/by reason of my limbs that are heavy').
segments.inferno-30-g114: ho io il braccio a tal mestiere sciolto». = 'I have my arm free for such a task.' Ho (present of avere) + past participle sciolto expresses a resultant state 'I have ... freed/loose' (= 'my arm is free/available'); io is emphatic; a tal mestiere = 'for such a job/purpose.'
segments.inferno-30-g115: Ond' ei rispuose: = 'Whereupon he replied:' Ond' (onde/ond) is a connective 'whereupon/therefore/thence'; ei is an archaic third-person pronoun 'he'; rispuose is passato remoto 'replied.'
segments.inferno-30-g116: «Quando tu andavi al fuoco, = 'When you used to go to the fire/forge,' with andavi (imperfect of andare) indicating habitual past action ('you would go/you used to go'), and al fuoco = 'to the fire' (the furnace/hearth).
segments.inferno-30-g117: non l'avei tu così presto; = 'you did not have it so quickly/readily.' L' is the clitic object 'it'; avei is imperfect of avere ('you had') used here to describe possession/availability in the past; così presto = 'so quickly' or 'so readily.'
segments.inferno-30-g118: ma sì e più l'avei quando coniavi». = 'but you had it as much and more when you were coining.' Ma = but; sì e più = 'so and more' = 'as much and even more'; l'avei repeats 'you had it'; quando coniavi (imperfect) 'when you were coining/minting' (habitual past).
segments.inferno-30-g119: E is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and' introducing the speaker's line; l' is the elided definite article (il) attached to the noun; idropico is a substantive (an epithet) meaning a person afflicted with dropsy — here used as a nominative label 'the dropsical man'. Word order is standard: conjunction + article + noun, functioning as the speaker tag before the direct speech.
segments.inferno-30-g120: Tu is the 2nd-person singular subject pronoun 'you'. Di’ is an archaic contracted form of the verb dire (cf. modern dici), 2nd-person singular present 'you say' (the apostrophe marks the loss of the final letter). Ver is the old form of vero 'true' used as a noun/adjective 'truthful' or 'true'. The second di is the preposition 'of/about' linking ver to questo; questo = 'this'. So the literal structure is 'You say true of this' = 'You speak the truth about this.'
segments.inferno-30-g121: ma = 'but'. tu non fosti = 'you were not', where fosti is the passato remoto (simple past) of essere used as a copula. sì = intensifier 'so'/'that much'. ver modifies testimonio: 'so true a witness' (i.e. not so truthful a witness). là 've is a fused locative: là ('there') + 've (from 'ove', 'where') = 'there where'. del ver = 'of the truth'. fosti ... richesto: here fosti is again passato remoto of essere used as auxiliary in a passive construction with the past participle richesto = 'asked/requested' — 'you were asked (for the truth)'. a Troia gives the location 'at Troy'. Word order is somewhat compressed: 'there where of the truth you were asked at Troy.'
segments.inferno-30-g122: S’io is an archaic contraction of se io (if I). Dissi is the passato remoto (first person singular) of dire, so literally “I said/spoke”; here it introduces a concessive protasis (“if I spoke…”). Falso functions as a predicate/adverbial meaning “falsely” or “untruly” (in older usage dire falso = to speak falsely).
segments.inferno-30-g123: E is the coordinating conjunction “and,” linking the two clauses. Tu falsasti is second person singular passato remoto (falsasti) with the subject pronoun tu given for emphasis: “you falsified/counterfeited.” Il conio is the direct object with the definite article; conio here means the coinage/mint (the die or production of coins).
segments.inferno-30-g124: Disse is third person singular passato remoto of dire (“he/she/it said”) and functions as the speech tag. Sinon is the proper name (nominative) of the speaker; the semicolon separates the tag from the next quoted clause.
segments.inferno-30-g125: E son qui uses son (an older short form of sono, first person singular present) plus qui “here”: “and I am here.” Per + noun (per un fallo) gives a causal sense “because of/on account of a fault/mistake,” with fallo meaning fault/mistake.
segments.inferno-30-g126: This clause is elliptical: the verb is omitted but understood from the context (“and you [are guilty]…”). Per più che alcun altro is a comparative construction: più … che = “more … than,” where ch’ is the contracted che “than,” and alcun altro = “any other.” Demonio is the comparison’s noun “demon” (i.e. more guilty than any other demon).
segments.inferno-30-g127: This segment is only a full stop (punctuation) marking the end of the preceding line/sentence; there are no lexical items to analyse.
segments.inferno-30-g128: Ricorditi is an imperative (or exhortative) form 'remember' with the enclitic reflexive pronoun -ti attached (modern ricordati). The form places the clitic directly on the verb, typical of older/poetic orthography.
segments.inferno-30-g129: spergiuro is a noun used vocatively/pejoratively: 'perjurer' or 'you perjurer' — an insult addressing the addressee for lying under oath.
segments.inferno-30-g130: del is the contraction of di + il 'of the'; cavallo = 'horse'. Together 'del cavallo' = 'of the horse' (here the Trojan Horse). The order is preposition + definite article + noun as usual.
segments.inferno-30-g131: rispuose is the passato remoto 'he answered'. quel = 'that one' (demonstrative pronoun) as subject. ch' (che) = relative pronoun 'who/which'. avëa is the archaic spelling of aveva (imperfect 'had'); infiata is a past participle 'inflated'/'puffed up' (from infiare, to inflate) agreeing with the feminine noun epa (paunch). l' is the elided article (la), epa = 'paunch'. The clause = 'he who had inflated the paunch answered.'
segments.inferno-30-g132: e = 'and'. sieti is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person present form of essere ('you are' — cf. modern sei); reo = 'guilty'/'culpable'. che introduces the following clause in the sense 'for'/'inasmuch as' or simply 'that' before the assertion. tutto il mondo = 'the whole world'. sallo is sapé + lo contracted: sa (he/it knows) + lo (it) = 'knows it' (here 'the whole world knows it'). The whole sentence: 'and you are guilty — as the whole world knows it.'
segments.inferno-30-g133: This segment contains only a full stop (punctuation). It functions as an end-of-sentence marker or a pause in the poem and has no lexical or grammatical material to analyze.
segments.inferno-30-g71: This is just a punctuation segment (the comma) connecting to the previous line; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-30-g72: Un gocciol d’acqua bramo puts the direct object before the verb for poetic emphasis. 'un' is the indefinite article (elided form of uno), 'gocciol' is a diminutive/poetic form for 'goccia' (a drop), 'd’' is the contracted preposition di, and 'bramo' is first person singular present indicative of bramare (‘I long for’), so the subject 'I' is omitted and supplied by the verb ending.
segments.inferno-30-g73: 'Li ruscelletti' is a masculine plural noun phrase with the (archaic) plural article 'Li'. The relative clause 'che d’i verdi colli del Casentin discendon giuso in Arno' modifies 'ruscelletti': 'che' is the relative pronoun, 'd’i' is the contraction of 'di i' = 'from the', 'verdi colli' = 'green hills', and 'discendon' is third-person plural present (poetic form -on) meaning 'they descend'; 'giuso in Arno' is an adverb + prepositional phrase indicating direction ('down into the Arno').
segments.inferno-30-g74: 'faccendo' (archaic spelling of facendo) is a present participle/gerund meaning 'making' or 'forming' and gives the circumstance for the streams; 'i lor canali' = 'their channels' (article + possessive 'lor'); 'freddi e molli' are coordinated adjectives describing those channels ('cold and soft/wet').
segments.inferno-30-g75: 'sempre' (always) is an adverb placed before the clitic pronoun and verb for emphasis; 'mi' is the indirect object/clitic 'to me'; 'stanno' is third-person plural present indicative of stare ('they stand/are situated'); 'innanzi' is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'in front (of)/before'. The whole line means 'they are always before me.'
segments.inferno-30-g76: A brief connective: 'e' = 'and', 'non' negates, and 'indarno' is an adverb meaning 'in vain' or 'to no purpose'.
segments.inferno-30-g77: 'ché' introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for' / 'because'). 'l’imagine lor' = 'their image/sight' (the article + noun + possessive), 'vie più' here functions adverbially ('ever more' / 'increasingly') modifying 'm’asciuga' ('dries me'); 'm’' is the clitic for 'mi' (me); 'asciuga' is third-person singular present of asciugare ('dries'). The comparative clause introduced by 'che' (= 'than') compares this drying to ' ’l male ond’ io nel volto mi discarno' — 'the harm/evil by which I strip the flesh from my face': 'ond’' = 'from which/whereby', 'discarno' is a present tense (poetic) verb meaning 'I strip (the) flesh' (make gaunt).
segments.inferno-30-g78: 'La rigida giustizia' is the subject noun phrase ('La' + adjective + noun). 'che mi fruga' is a relative clause: 'che' = 'which/that', 'mi' = 'me' (direct object clitic), 'fruga' = present indicative of frugare meaning 'searches/scrutinizes'. The main verb 'tragge' (archaic spelling of trae/tragge) is third-person singular present meaning 'draws/takes', governing 'cagion' (cause/reason) and the complement 'del loco ov’ io peccai' ('from the place where I sinned'). The infinitive phrase 'a metter più li miei sospiri in fuga' expresses purpose/result: 'a' + infinitive 'metter' = 'to put', 'più' = 'more', 'li miei sospiri' = 'my sighs' with plural article, and 'in fuga' = 'to flight/into rout'.
segments.inferno-30-g79: 'Ivi è Romena' is a locative clause: 'Ivi' = 'there', 'è' = third-person singular present of essere ('is' / 'is located'), and 'Romena' is the place-name. The phrasing simply states that Romena is located there.
segments.inferno-30-g80: 'là dov’ io falsai' = 'there where I falsified' ('là' = 'there'; 'dov’' contraction of 'dove' = 'where'; 'io falsai' = first person singular passato remoto 'I falsified/counterfeited'). 'la lega suggellata del Batista' is the object: 'la lega' = 'the league/agreement', 'suggellata' = past participle 'sealed', and 'del Batista' = 'of the Batista/Baptist' (proper name or title).
segments.inferno-30-g81: 'per ch’io' here introduces the reason/result ('for which/wherefore I...'). 'il corpo sù arso' is a participial phrase: 'il corpo' = 'the body', 'sù' = adverb 'up' (intensive), 'arso' = past participle 'burned'. 'lasciai' is first person singular passato remoto of lasciare ('I left/abandoned'). So literally, 'for which reason I left my body burned up.'
segments.inferno-30-g134: This is a short curse phrased as an optative: 'E' (and) opens the clause; 'te' is the dative/ethical object 'to you'; 'sia' is the present subjunctive of essere used here with an optative force ('may ... be'); 'rea' is a predicate adjective agreeing with 'la sete' (feminine) meaning 'guilty' or 'to blame'; the noun phrase 'la sete' ('the thirst') is the grammatical subject. The relative adverb 'onde' introduces the clause 'onde ti crepa' ('from/whereby you burst'), with 'ti' the clitic object and 'crepa' an archaic/poetic present-form (here best read as describing the effect: 'that makes you burst').
segments.inferno-30-g135: 'disse' is the passato remoto (simple past) third-person singular of 'dire' ('said'), used to report speech; '’l' is the elided article 'il' (the) before 'Greco', so the whole is a standard narrator tag placed between parts of the direct quotation.
segments.inferno-30-g136: This begins the resumed quotation after the speaker-tag: the article 'la' + noun 'lingua' stands alone as an exclamatory/elliptical item in the curse — i.e. 'and the tongue' — supplying another thing the speaker invokes as part of the malediction. The noun can mean either the physical tongue or speech; here it functions concretely as an item being cursed.
segments.inferno-30-g137: 'e' (and) links this phrase to the previous 'la lingua'; 'l’ acqua marcia' = 'the rotten water' (article + noun + adjective). 'che' is a relative pronoun 'which/that' introducing the relative clause. The phrase 'l' ventre innanzi a li occhi' ('the belly before the eyes') is the object/affected part, with the prepositional phrase 'innanzi a li occhi' ('before your eyes') placed in the middle for emphasis. 'sì' is an intensifier 'so', and 't’ assiepa' is the verb with the clitic 't’' ('you') attached: 'assiepa' (archaic form of 'assieprare/assiepare') means 'crowds/piles up/presses', so the whole relative clause means 'that so crowds/piles up the belly before your eyes (on you)'. Note the clitic pronoun attaches to the verb in the usual medieval/poetic order.
segments.inferno-30-g138: This segment is likewise only a punctuation mark (a full stop). It marks the close of a line or sentence and carries no grammatical words to interpret.
segments.inferno-30-g139: Allora is an adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'; il monetier is the subject phrase 'the moneyer' (monetier is a noun borrowed from a medieval occupational term). The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-30-g140: Così is an adverb 'thus/so'; si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic used to form a passive-like or impersonal construction ('is torn'); squarcia is present indicative (third singular) of squarciare 'to tear open/split'; la bocca tua = 'your mouth' (la + noun + possessive after the noun, a normal placement in Italian poetry); per tuo mal = 'because of/for your evil/ill-doing' (per here marks cause); come suole = 'as is customary' (come + verb suole, where suole is the normal present of suolare 'be accustomed').
segments.inferno-30-g141: ché is a conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because', introducing the explanatory subordinate clause that follows.
segments.inferno-30-g142: s’i’ is a contraction for se io ('if I') in older/poetic Italian (here introducing a conditional); ho is first-person present of avere 'I have'; sete is a noun 'thirst'; e links the coordinated clause; omor (older spelling of 'umor'/'humor') means bodily fluid or 'blood'/'humor' that 'fills' someone; mi is the clitic object 'me'; rinfarcia is present tense of rinfarciare 'fills/crams' (here 'fills me').
segments.inferno-30-g143: tu hai = 'you have' (present indicative of avere); l’ arsura = 'the burning/thirst' (arsura implies burning heat or parching thirst); e = 'and'; ’l capo = 'the head' (capo); che ti duole = relative clause 'that pains you' (duole = 'hurts' in present indicative, ti is the dative/accusative clitic 'you').
segments.inferno-30-g144: e = 'and'; per + infinitive (per leccar) introduces purpose or intent here best read as 'to' or 'in order to lick'; leccar is the infinitive 'to lick' (poetic form of leccare); lo specchio di Narcisso = 'the mirror of Narcissus' (lo + noun + di + proper name).
segments.inferno-30-g145: non = negation 'not'; vorresti is the conditional present (second person singular) of volere 'would you want/wish'; a + nvitar (nvitar = invitare, archaic spelling) = 'to invite/summon' (here 'to call forth' or 'to utter'); molte parole = 'many words' (molte modifies parole). The conditional softens the reproach: 'you would not wish to summon many words.'
segments.inferno-30-g146: Ad + infinitive introduces the activity and here functions like an English gerund phrase 'on listening to them.' 'Ascoltarli' is the infinitive + clitic object 'li' (them). 'Er’' is an archaic contracted form of 'ero' (I was). The subject pronoun 'io' follows the verb (inverted for poetic order), and 'del tutto' is an idiom meaning 'completely' modifying the adjective 'fisso' (= fixed/intent).
segments.inferno-30-g147: 'Quando' introduces the temporal clause 'when.' The article 'l'' is the elided form of 'il' before 'maestro' (the master/guide). 'Mi' is the indirect-object clitic 'to me' placed before the verb, and 'disse' is passato remoto (he said), used here to introduce direct speech.
segments.inferno-30-g148: 'Or' = 'now' and 'pur' is an emphatic particle (here roughly 'then' / 'indeed'), together forming an idiomatic opening to an imperative. 'Mira' is the 2nd-person singular imperative of 'mirare' meaning 'look' or 'gaze (at)' addressed to the narrator.
segments.inferno-30-g149: This subordinate clause expresses an almost-event: 'che' introduces it; 'per poco' ('by a little') + the following 'che' give the sense 'for only a little / almost.' 'Teco' = 'with you.' The negation 'non' with 'mi risso' (reflexive construction 'mi' + verb) means 'I did not bristle/flare up'—here 'risso' is an archaic/poetic past or perfect-form meaning to stand up/bristle (i.e., get angry). The whole clause: 'for a little I had not bristled with you.'
segments.inferno-30-g30: E is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. l’ is the article il with elision before a vowel. Aretin is a dialectal/poetic form meaning 'Aretine' (a person from Arezzo). che rimase is a relative clause with rimase as passato remoto of rimanere meaning 'who remained / who stayed behind.'
segments.inferno-30-g31: tremando is a gerund (present participle) meaning 'trembling' and modifies the subject; mi is the indirect object 'to me'; disse is passato remoto of dire, 'said.' The sequence places the gerund before the main verb for vividness.
segments.inferno-30-g32: Quel is the demonstrative 'that'; folletto is a diminutive/nickname meaning 'imp' or 'little spirit'; è is present indicative 'is' linking subject and predicate; Gianni Schicchi is the proper name (predicate nominative).
segments.inferno-30-g33: e is 'and' joining clauses. va is the present of andare used idiomatically with a gerund to mean 'goes about' or 'behaves by doing X'. rabbioso is an adjective modifying the subject (he goes about 'rabid'/'furious'). altrui means 'towards others' / 'against others'. così modifies the gerund conciando; conciando is the gerund of conciare meaning 'treat/make up/handle' (here 'treat roughly' or 'maltreat'), showing how he acts toward others.
segments.inferno-30-g35: diss’ is a contracted poetic form of disse (passato remoto 'said'); subject io follows the verb by poetical inversion; lui is the indirect object 'to him' (I said to him).
segments.inferno-30-g36: se introduces a conditional 'if' (here used colloquially). l’altro 'the other' is the subject; non ti ficchi combines negation + reflexive/indirect pronoun ti + ficchi (ficchare/ficcare) meaning 'to thrust/sink (in)' — here 'sink his teeth into you.' li denti is literal 'the teeth' (direct object); a dosso is a fixed phrase 'onto/against (your) back' meaning 'into you.' Word order places the object (li denti) after the verb as the phrase 'ficchi li denti a dosso'.
segments.inferno-30-g37: non ti sia fatica is an impersonal/idiomatic construction: non + (indirect) ti + sia (present subjunctive of essere) + fatica 'difficulty' — 'it will not be a trouble for you'. a dir is the infinitive 'to say/tell' governed by fatica; chi è is the indirect question 'who he is' (chi 'who', è present indicative).
segments.inferno-30-g38: pria che is a temporal conjunction meaning 'before'; di qui 'from here'; si spicchi uses the reflexive si with spicchi (present subjunctive of spiccare) after a temporal expression — 'before he detach(s) himself / breaks away from here.' The subjunctive is standard after 'pria che' to mark anticipated action.
segments.inferno-30-g39: Ed is a variant of e 'and' used for euphony. elli is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'he' (i.e. 'he said'); a me marks the indirect object 'to me'. The short clause 'Ed elli a me:' is an elliptical way to introduce reported speech.
segments.inferno-30-g40: Quell’ is the demonstrative 'that'; è is present 'is'; l’ anima antica is 'the ancient soul' (article + noun + adjective). di Mirra names the person (Mirra/Myrrha); scellerata is an adjective 'wicked' modifying Mirra. Note Mirra is a proper name here (mythological figure), though the lemma list gives the common noun 'myrrh.'
segments.inferno-30-g41: che introduces a relative clause 'who'; divenne is passato remoto of divenire 'became'; al padre is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the father' (i.e. toward her father). The clause states the relation formed.
segments.inferno-30-g42: fuor (archaic for fuori) del dritto amore means 'outside of rightful/lawful love'; fuor del indicates being 'outside of' something; dritto modifies amore to mean 'right/legitimate love' (i.e. not a proper consented love).
segments.inferno-30-g43: amica is a feminine noun meaning 'female friend' or 'female lover' in this context (she became a lover). Positioned at the end as a predicative adjective/noun completing the relative clause.
segments.inferno-30-g44: Questa 'this one' (feminine) is the subject. a peccar uses the preposition a + infinitive peccar (poetic infinitive of peccare) with venne (see below) to express 'came to sin' or 'came into sin'; con esso 'with him/it' indicates the partner; così 'thus' qualifies the manner; venne is passato remoto of venire 'came.' The construction 'venne a + infinitive' expresses purpose or result.
segments.inferno-30-g45: falsificando is a gerund 'falsifying' and describes how she acted; sé is the reflexive pronoun 'herself'; in altrui forma literally 'into another's form' (altrui = 'belonging to another'); forma = 'form/appearance.' The gerund links to venne: 'she came falsifying herself into another's form.'
segments.inferno-30-g46: come introduces a comparison 'as'; l’altro 'the other one' is the subject; che là sen va is a relative clause: là 'there', sen is the old clitic form of 'ne' combined with si (here 'sen va' = 'se ne va' or 'goes off/away'), va is present of andare. The phrase describes 'the other who goes off yonder.'
segments.inferno-30-g47: sostenne is passato remoto of sostenere used parenthetically to mean 'he maintained/claimed' (i.e. the other asserted this). It introduces the reported purpose clause that follows.
segments.inferno-30-g48: per + infinitive guadagnar expresses purpose 'in order to gain/win'; la donna de la torma 'the woman of the troop' (de la torma = of the group/company). The whole phrase gives the motive: 'in order to win the woman of the company.'
segments.inferno-30-g49: falsificare is the infinitive 'to falsify/forge/impersonate'; in sé 'in himself/in his person' indicates impersonation; Buoso Donati is the name of the man being impersonated. The infinitive states the action intended (to impersonate Buoso Donati).
segments.inferno-30-g50: testando and dando are gerunds ('making a will' and 'giving') that complete the infinitive falsificare, describing the means: 'by drawing up a will and by giving the will its rule/terms' (testamento = will; norma = rule/terms). The gerunds indicate simultaneous actions supporting the impersonation/fraud.
segments.inferno-30-g168: Fa ragion is an impersonal expression literally “makes reason” = “it is reasonable.” The subordinate introduced by ch’ (that) contains a present subjunctive (sia) because the speaker states what is proper or fitting; ch’io ti sia sempre allato = “that I be always beside you.” The clitic ti precedes the finite verb sia (normal proclitic placement), and allato is a predicative adjective/participle meaning “at one’s side, beside.”
segments.inferno-30-g169: This conditional clause begins with se (if) and the idiom più avvien che (“if it should ever happen that...”), where avvien is impersonal (“it happens/befalls”) and introduces a subjunctive clause (accoglia). The clitic t’ = ti is attached to accoglia (t’accoglia = receives you). Dove + subjunctive sien expresses a potential location (“where there may be people”) and in simigliante piato is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly “in a similar condition/disposition” (piato is archaic/poetic).
segments.inferno-30-g170: Ché is causal (“for/because”). Voler + infinitive (ciò udire) is a noun-like construction meaning “the wanting/wish to hear that”; udire is the infinitive governed by voler. The predicate è bassa voglia states a judgement: bassa (agreeing adjective) qualifies voglia (a feminine noun, “desire”), hence “is a base/low desire.”
segments.inferno-30-g95: "E io a lui:" is an elliptical speech-introduction: E (and) links the line to what came before, io is the first-person subject 'I', and a lui marks the indirect object 'to him' (i.e., 'I said to him'). The verb of saying is omitted because the following direct quotation supplies the speech.
segments.inferno-30-g96: This is the beginning of the direct question. Chi son is inverted 'who are' (son = 3rd-person plural or 1st-person singular archaic, here plural 'are'). li due tapini = 'the two poor wretches' (li + numeral + noun). che fumman introduces a relative clause: che = 'who/that' and fumman is an archaic present form (3rd pl.) meaning 'they smoke/steam/emit vapour'. come man bagnate ’l verno is a simile: come = 'like', man = 'hands', bagnate = past participle used adjectivally 'wet', and ’l verno = 'the winter' (il verno, elided).
segments.inferno-30-g97: giacendo is a present gerund (lying/being situated) describing the two persons' posture. stretti is an adjectival past participle agreeing in masculine plural with 'li due tapini' and means 'pressed/tight together'. a’ tuoi destri confini is a prepositional phrase 'against/on your right-hand borders' (a + tuoi = at/to your, destri = 'right-hand', confini = 'boundaries').
segments.inferno-30-g98: A single punctuation mark (a period) that closes the preceding line of dialogue; here it functions as the printed end of the question before the next speaker replies.
segments.inferno-30-g99: Qui li trovai is a simple past (passato remoto) clause: 'I found them here' (Qui = here; li = them; trovai = I found). The parenthetical e poi volta non dierno is an archaic remark set off by dashes: e poi = 'and afterwards', volta = 'turned' (used nominally), non = 'not', while dierno is uncertain/archaic in form here (see senses). The whole parenthesis means roughly 'and afterwards they did not turn/return'.
segments.inferno-30-g100: rispuose is the 3rd-person singular passato remoto of rispondere: 'he answered' (narrative tag identifying the speaker of the quotation).
segments.inferno-30-g101: quando piovvi in questo greppo is a temporal clause: quando = 'when'; piovvi is an archaic past form here meaning 'I fell' or 'I was cast down' (the verb is used for the speaker's falling into the place); in questo greppo = 'into/on this steep bank or slope' (greppo = crag/steep bank).
segments.inferno-30-g102: e non credo che dieno in sempiterno is a subordinate clause expressing doubt: e non credo che = 'and I do not believe that', dieno is an archaic/variant form whose precise force is uncertain in this manuscript tradition (contextually 'they will remain' or 'they will be there' is intended), in = 'in', sempiterno = 'eternity/everlasting'. The clause therefore conveys 'and I don't believe they will be there for ever.'
segments.inferno-30-g103: L’una è la falsa is 'The one is the false woman' or 'one of them is the false one' (L' = the; una = one; è = is; la falsa = the deceitful/false woman). ch’accusò Gioseppo is a relative clause: ch’ = 'who/that' and accusò is passato remoto 'accused' (he/she accused) with Gioseppo as object.
segments.inferno-30-g104: l’altr’ è ’l falso Sinon greco di Troia states the identity of the other: l’altr' = 'the other', è = 'is', ’l falso Sinon = 'the false Sinon' (falso as substantive/adjective), greco = 'a Greek' modifying Sinon, di Troia = 'of Troy' (i.e., from Troy). Word order is straightforward: article + adjective + name + appositive qualifiers.
segments.inferno-30-g105: per febbre aguta gittan tanto leppo is a causal-result clause: per febbre aguta = 'because of an acute fever' (per expresses cause), gittan is 3rd-person plural present 'they throw/emit/let off' (archaic form of gettan), tanto = 'so much/such a great amount', and leppo = 'foul stench' (object); overall: 'because of an acute fever they emit such a stench.'
segments.inferno-30-g82: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'. S’io is an archaic contraction of se io ('if I'); vedessi is the imperfect-subjunctive (congiuntivo imperfetto) of vedere used in the protasis of a conditional to express a hypothetical or counterfactual situation. Qui is a locative adverb 'here'; l’anima trista is the noun phrase 'the sad/wretched soul' (l’ is the elided article before a vowel, and trista is an adjective after the noun). The sequence di Guido o d’Alessandro o di lor frate are successive prepositional phrases naming persons (lor is the archaic form of loro, 'their').
segments.inferno-30-g83: Per Fonte Branda is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for Fonte Branda' (here implying 'even in exchange for the place/sight called Fonte Branda'). Non darei is the first-person singular conditional present ('I would not give'), with non negating the verb; la vista is the direct object 'the sight' or 'the view' (i.e. seeing that soul).
segments.inferno-30-g84: Dentro 'inside' gives the location; c’ is the clitic/particle ci (here functioning as a locative 'there'); è is the present indicative of essere 'is'; l’una means 'the one' (one of them, feminine), and già 'already' modifies the state. Together they assert that one (person) is already inside.
segments.inferno-30-g85: Se introduces a subordinate conditional sense 'if'; l’arrabbiate ombre is a noun phrase 'the enraged/angry shades' (l’ is an elided plural article used by Dante before a vowel, arrabbiate agrees with ombre). Che vanno intorno is a relative clause 'that go about/are moving around' (vanno is present indicative of andare); dicon vero is a shortened poetic form of dicon(o) 'they say' with vero as predicative adjective 'true' — 'if the enraged shades that roam around speak truly.'
segments.inferno-30-g86: Ma 'but' begins a contrast; che here is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what' or 'that' in the formula 'ma che mi val' meaning 'but what good/use is it to me'; mi is the indirect object 'to me'; val is present indicative of valere 'it is worth / it avails' (here third person singular impersonal: 'what use is it').
segments.inferno-30-g87: C’ho is the colloquial fused form of ci + ho (here functioning as the first-person present 'I have'); le membra is the direct object 'the limbs' (le = the plural feminine article); legate is the past participle used adjectivally 'bound/tied' agreeing with membra. The question mark signals a rhetorical question emphasizing his inability to move.
segments.inferno-30-g88: S’io (se io) introduces the conditional 'if I'; fossi is the imperfect-subjunctive of essere 'were' used in the protasis to express a hypothetical. Pur, di tanto, ancor qualify leggero ('even, to such an extent, still light/nimble'). Ch’ (che) introduces the subordinate clause 'that I might be able' and i’ potessi uses the imperfect-subjunctive of potere ('that I could'), followed by the infinitive phrase in cent’ anni andare un’oncia 'to go an ounce/inch in a hundred years' — a hyperbolic way to say 'even if I could move extremely little over a long time.'
segments.inferno-30-g89: Io sarei messo già combines the conditional auxiliary sarei ('I would be') with the past participle messo ('placed/put'), yielding 'I would already be placed' (i.e. I would already have reached). Per lo sentiero ('along the path') indicates the route; già reinforces 'already.' This is the apodosis (result clause) of the conditional introduced earlier.
segments.inferno-30-g90: Cercando is a gerund ('searching'/'seeking') describing what he would be doing; lui is the direct object 'him' (the person he seeks). Tra questa gente sconcia means 'among these disreputable/filthy people' (tra = among, gente = people, sconcia = derogatory adjective). The gerund links to the conditional result: 'I would be set on the path, searching for him among these people.'
segments.inferno-30-g91: Con tutto ch’ella volge undici miglia is a concessive clause introduced by 'con tutto che' meaning 'although' or 'even though.' Ella is a feminine pronoun used impersonally here (referring to the place or circuit just mentioned), volge is present indicative of volgere 'turns/extends/encircles', and undici miglia is the distance 'eleven miles.'
segments.inferno-30-g92: E introduces an additional statement. Men d’un mezzo di traverso non ci ha literally 'it has not even less than half across' — idiomatically 'it does not have even half a mile across.' Men (meno) = 'less'; d’un mezzo di traverso = 'of half (a mile) across'; non ci ha is literally 'it does not have there' (idiomatic 'there is not / it has not'). The construction states that the place's crosswise extent is under half a mile.
segments.inferno-30-g93: Io son 'I am'; per lor here means 'among/with them' or 'on their account' (per + pronoun); tra sì fatta famiglia literally 'among such a made family' means 'among such a company/household' — sì fatta is a past-participial adjective phrase 'thus made/constituted' modifying famiglia. The whole states his membership in that disreputable group.
segments.inferno-30-g94: E’ m’indussero is e (and) plus the passé remoto indussero ('they induced/led me') with the clitic m’ = 'me' attached to the verb. A batter li fiorini is an infinitival complement 'to beat/mint the florins' (batter can mean to mint or to counterfeit), with li fiorini 'the florins' as object. Ch’avevan tre carati di mondiglia is a relative clause 'which had three carats of dross' (avevan = avevano, imperfect 'they had'; carati = units of fineness; mondiglia = dross/impurity), explaining that the coins were debased.
segments.inferno-30-g1: 'Nel' is the contraction of in + il ('in the'); 'tempo che' introduces a temporal clause meaning 'at the time when.' 'Iunone' is the subject (Juno). 'era crucciata' is a descriptive past state: era (imperfect of essere, 'was') + the participial/adjectival crucciata ('upset/angry'), so 'was angry.' 'per Semelè' indicates the cause/target ('about/against Semele'), and 'contra ’l sangue tebano' uses the preposition contra ('against') with 'sangue tebano' meaning 'Theban blood/lineage.'
segments.inferno-30-g2: 'come' = 'as' (introducing a comparison/example). 'mostrò' is passato remoto of mostrare ('showed/demonstrated') referring back to things that have shown this before. 'una e altra fïata' literally 'one and the other time' = 'each time' or 'on each occasion' (una = one, altra = other, fïata = archaic for 'time/occasion').
segments.inferno-30-g3: Subject 'Atamante' (Athamas) + passato remoto 'divenne' (become) = 'became.' 'tanto insano' is the result complement 'so/so very insane.'
segments.inferno-30-g4: 'che' introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that/that'). 'veggendo' is a present participle/gerund (archaic veggere for vedere) meaning 'seeing' or 'on seeing.' 'la moglie' is the direct object 'the wife' (here his wife). 'con due figli andar carcata' uses 'andar' + past participle 'carcata' to mean 'going/being laden' — i.e. 'carrying two children' — and 'da ciascuna mano' specifies 'each by a hand' (each child held by one hand).
segments.inferno-30-g5: 'gridò' is passato remoto of gridare, third person singular: 'he cried/shouted,' introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-30-g6: 'Tendiam' is an archaic/poetic first‑person‑plural exhortation ('tendiamo' in modern Italian) meaning 'let us stretch/let us set up.' 'le reti' is the direct object 'the nets.'
segments.inferno-30-g7: 'sì ch’io pigli' = 'so that I may take/catch' — 'sì che' introduces purpose/result and 'pigli' is present subjunctive of pigliare ('that I catch'). 'la leonessa e ' leoncini' are the objects ('the lioness and the lion‑cubs'). 'al varco' = 'at the pass/entrance' (al = a + il).
segments.inferno-30-g8: 'e poi' = 'and then.' 'distese' is passato remoto of distendere ('stretched out'). 'i dispietati artigli' = 'the merciless claws/talons' (i + adjective + noun).
segments.inferno-30-g9: 'prendendo' is a gerund ('taking/seizing'). 'l’un' = 'the one' / 'one of them' (elided form). 'ch’avea nome Learco' is a relative clause: ch’ (che) = 'who' and 'avea' is imperfect of avere ('had') + 'nome' = 'name' — 'who had the name Learchus.'
segments.inferno-30-g10: Sequence of actions in the past: 'e rotollo' (rotollo = passato remoto of rotolare, 'and he rolled [him]') and 'e percosselo' is 'and struck him' (percosselo = percosse + enclitic lo), 'ad un sasso' = 'against a rock.'
segments.inferno-30-g11: 'e quella' = 'and that one' (i.e. the woman). 's’annegò' is reflexive passato remoto of annegare ('she drowned herself'). 'con l’altro carco' = 'with the other load/burden' (i.e. with the other child). 'carco' is an adjective meaning 'laden/burdened.'
segments.inferno-30-g12: 'E quando' = 'And when.' 'la fortuna' is personified 'Fortune/fate.' 'volse' is passato remoto of volgere ('turned'); 'in basso' = 'downward/low.' 'l’altezza de’ Troian' = 'the loftiness/height of the Trojans.' The relative 'che tutto ardiva' ('who dared everything') uses 'ardiva' (imperfect of ardire) to describe them.
segments.inferno-30-g13: 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that'). '’nsieme col regno' = 'together with the kingdom.' 'il re fu casso' — 'fu' (past of essere) + 'casso' (archaic) conveys that the king was brought low/overthrown/removed along with the realm; the verb form is passato remoto/passive resultive in a poetic register.
segments.inferno-30-g14: Proper name 'Ecuba' (Hecuba), here nominative subject of the following attributes/actions.
segments.inferno-30-g15: Three coordinating adjectives ('misera e cattiva') giving qualities of Ecuba: 'misera' = wretched/pitiable; 'cattiva' = bad/wretched (here emphasizing her unhappy condition).
segments.inferno-30-g16: 'poscia che' = 'after' / 'afterwards that.' 'vide' is passato remoto of vedere ('she saw'). 'Polissena morta' = 'Polyxena dead.' This clause establishes the trigger for her grief.
segments.inferno-30-g17: 'e del suo Polidoro in su la riva del mar' = 'and (of) her Polydorus upon the shore of the sea.' 'si fu la dolorosa accorta' is a reflexive/periphrastic perfect: 'si' (reflexive) + 'fu' (past of essere) + 'accorta' (past participle/adj 'aware'), so 'the sorrowful woman became aware/realised' — word order is poetic, with 'la dolorosa' as a descriptive apposition.
segments.inferno-30-g18: 'forsennata' (past participle used adjectivally) = 'made mad, frenzied.' 'latrò' is passato remoto of latrare ('barked/howled'); 'sì come cane' = 'just like a dog' (simile introduced by 'come').
segments.inferno-30-g19: 'tanto' = 'so much.' 'il dolor le fé' uses 'fé' (old form of fece, passato remoto of fare) = 'did/made to her' — 'grief did to her.' 'la mente torta' = 'the mind crooked/warped' — 'torta' is adjectival past participle 'twisted,' so 'grief so warped her mind.'
segments.inferno-30-g20: 'Ma né ... né' = 'But neither ... nor.' 'di Tebe furie' = 'Furies/ferocious women of Thebes' (poetic reference). 'troiane' = Trojan women. 'si vider mäi' is an archaic inversion: 'si vider mai' = 'were ever seen' (passive perception); 'in alcun tanto crude' = 'in any such degree/so cruel.'
segments.inferno-30-g21: Negative general statement: 'non punger bestie' = 'they do not sting/attack beasts.' 'non' negates the verb 'punger' (infinitive here used as a kind of general statement) and 'bestie' = 'beasts.'
segments.inferno-30-g22: 'nonché' here functions as 'let alone' / 'not to mention' (emphatic escalation). 'membra umane' = 'human limbs/bodies' (i.e. they would not attack even animals, much less humans).
segments.inferno-30-g23: 'quant’ io vidi' = 'as much as I saw' / 'that which I saw.' 'in due ombre smorte e nude' = 'in two shades/ghosts, pale (smorte = lifeless/pale) and naked' — 'io vidi' is passato remoto 'I saw' giving the eyewitness statement.
segments.inferno-30-g24: Relative clause: 'che mordendo correvan' = 'who, biting, ran about' — 'mordendo' gerund 'biting,' 'correvan' imperfect 'they ran/ran around.' 'di quel modo' = 'in that way,' and the simile 'che ’l porco quando del porcil si schiude' = 'as the pig when it comes out of the sty' (si schiude = 'opens itself/comes out').
segments.inferno-30-g25: 'L’una' = 'the one (one of them).' 'giunse' is passato remoto of giungere ('arrived/reached'). 'a Capocchio' marks the person reached — Capocchio is used as a proper name (also meaning 'blockhead' as a nickname), here the victim.
segments.inferno-30-g26: 'e in sul nodo del collo' = 'and on the knot of the neck' (in sul is an archaic double preposition meaning 'on the'). 'l’assannò' = 'she bit/mawed him' — 'assannò' is passato remoto of assannare ('to maul, bite savagely'), with the clitic l' = 'him' attached to the verb.
segments.inferno-30-g27: 'sì che' introduces a result clause ('so that/with the result that'). This short segment connects the biting action to its consequence.
segments.inferno-30-g28: 'tirando' is a gerund ('pulling'); here it explains the manner in which the consequence occurred (i.e. by pulling/tugging).
segments.inferno-30-g29: Causative construction: 'grattar li fece il ventre' literally 'she made (to) him scratch the belly' — 'grattar' (infinitive 'to scratch'), 'li' = clitic 'to him/him,' 'fece' = passato remoto of fare ('made'), then 'il ventre al fondo sodo' = 'the belly to the hard/firm bottom' — overall: by pulling she made his belly be scraped down to the firm bottom. Word order is inverted for poetic effect (infinitive + clitic + fare + patient).
segments.inferno-30-g150: This segment is just a punctuation mark (a full stop) in the text—there are no words or grammatical elements to analyze.
segments.inferno-30-g151: Quand' is the elided form of quando ('when'); the clause uses the speaker as subject (io) and senti' is a poetically written past tense (passato remoto / poetic elision of sentii or sentì) meaning 'I heard'. The clitic 'l' stands for lo ('him'/'it') placed before the verb. The infinitive phrase a me parlar ('to speak to me') follows senti' and con ira is an adverbial phrase 'with anger'.
segments.inferno-30-g152: Volsimi is the enclitic form of volsi + mi (volgere, passato remoto) meaning 'I turned (myself)'; the reflexive clitic mi is attached. Verso lui is a prepositional phrase 'toward him', and con tal vergogna is a manner phrase 'with such shame' that modifies the turning.
segments.inferno-30-g153: Ch' is a contraction of che introducing a result/consequence ('so that'); ancor = 'still' or 'even now'; per la memoria = 'by/through the memory' (per + definite article + noun). The verb phrase mi si gira uses two clitics (mi + si) before the verb gira: this reflexive/impersonal construction yields 'I turn / I am turned' in sense 'I am moved/affected by the memory.'
segments.inferno-30-g154: Qual è colui che ... is a formula beginning a simile: 'Such is the one who...' Colui is a demonstrative/identifier 'the one' (here 'the man who'); che is the relative pronoun introducing the subordinate clause; suo dannaggio is a possessive + noun 'his own damage/ruin'; sogna is present indicative ('dreams').
segments.inferno-30-g155: This is a relative continuation: che + gerund sognando ('while dreaming') modifies the subject, and desidera + infinitive sognare expresses 'desires to dream' — desidera is present indicative third person singular and sognare is its infinitive complement.
segments.inferno-30-g156: Sì che functions together as a resultive conjunction 'so that'; quel ch’è is a small clause 'that which is' (quel + relative ch' + è), with the relative 'ch'' = che meaning 'which' or 'that which'.
segments.inferno-30-g157: Come non fosse uses come ('as' / 'as if') plus the imperfect subjunctive fosse ('were') to create a contrary-to-fact or hypothetical comparison: 'as if it were not.' Non negates the verb.
segments.inferno-30-g158: Agogna is the third-person singular present of agognare ('to yearn for/long for'), agreeing with the subject in the previous clause (that which exists is longed for as if it did not exist).
segments.inferno-30-g159: Tal mi fec' io is an inversion where fec' is the apocopated passato remoto (feci) 'I made/did', with the enclitic mi meaning 'myself' (reflexive). The subject io follows the verb for emphasis: 'thus did I make myself' / 'so it was with me.'
segments.inferno-30-g160: Non possendo parlare is a circumstantial clause using the gerund (possendo) with non to mean 'not being able' or 'unable to speak'; parlare is the infinitive complement.
segments.inferno-30-g161: Che introduces a result/consequence clause: disïava is the imperfect 'I longed/wished', followed by the infinitive scusarmi (scusare + enclitic mi) 'to excuse myself' — showing desire with an object in the reflexive form.
segments.inferno-30-g162: E scusava me tuttavia uses the imperfect scusava ('kept excusing') with dislocated object me for emphasis; tuttavia here means 'continually' / 'still' in the sense of 'kept on doing so'.
segments.inferno-30-g163: E nol mi credea fare: nol is the contracted form of non lo (negative + direct object pronoun) and the clause with credea (imperfect 'he/she/it believed') plus infinitive fare means 'and he did not believe me (to be) able to do it' — i.e. 'he didn't believe me capable of doing it.'
segments.inferno-30-g164: This is a terse proverb-like clause with inverted word order: 'Maggior difetto' (a greater fault) is the grammatical subject, 'men vergogna' ('less shame' — 'men' is the archaic comparative for 'meno') is the object, and 'lava' is the 3rd-person singular present indicative of 'lavare' (literally 'washes'). The line therefore reads 'A greater fault washes away less shame' (i.e. a graver offense makes lesser shame disappear).
segments.inferno-30-g165: 'Disse' (passato remoto 'he said') introduces the reported speech; the article 'l'' (il) before 'maestro' is elided in the usual poetic contraction.
segments.inferno-30-g166: Here 'che' functions causally/introducing an explanatory clause ('for'/'since'). ''L' tuo' is the article + possessive ('your'), a normal medieval/poetic usage to place the article before the possessive. 'Non è stato' is a perfect-like construction (auxiliary 'è' + past participle 'stato') meaning 'was not' or 'has not been'—i.e. 'yours was not (such a greater fault)'.
segments.inferno-30-g167: 'Però' = 'therefore' links the conclusion to the previous clause. 'D'' is the contracted form of 'di' before 'ogne' (poetic form of 'ogni' = every), so 'd'ogne trestizia' = 'of every sorrow/sadness.' The clitic 'ti' (to you) precedes the verb 'disgrava' (3rd-person present), so the literal order is 'therefore of every sorrow it [something] you'—contextually meaning 'therefore it relieves/exempts you from every sorrow.'

sentences.inferno-30-s9: And after the two furious ones had passed, over whom I had kept my eye, I turned it back to look at the other ill-born.
sentences.inferno-30-s10: I saw one, made in the likeness of a lute, although he had had his groin cut off by the other that man has forked.
sentences.inferno-30-s11: The grievous dropsy, which so disfigures the limbs with the humour that ill transforms them, so that the face does not correspond to the belly, made him hold his lips open like the consumptive does, who, because of thirst, turns one lip toward the chin and the other upward.
sentences.inferno-30-s12: "O you who are without any suffering, and I do not know why, in this wretched world," he said to us, "look and pay attention to the misery of master Adam; while I was alive I had much of what I wanted, and now, alas!"
sentences.inferno-30-s25: And one of them, who, perhaps annoyed at being called so obscure, struck him with his fist on the coarse paunch.
sentences.inferno-30-s26: That sounded as if it were a drum; and Master Adam struck his face with his arm, which did not seem any less hard, saying to him: "Although the power to move has been taken from me because my limbs are heavy, I have my arm free for such a task."
sentences.inferno-30-s27: Whereupon he answered: "When you used to go to the fire, you did not have it so quickly; but you had it as much and more when you were coining."
sentences.inferno-30-s28: And the dropsical man: "You speak the truth about this; but you were not so true a witness where the truth was demanded at Troy."
sentences.inferno-30-s29: “If I spoke falsely, and you falsified the coin,” said Sinon; “and I am here for one fault, and you for more than any other demon!”
sentences.inferno-30-s31: "Remember, perjurer, the horse," answered he who had inflated his paunch; "and you are guilty — the whole world knows it!"
sentences.inferno-30-s13: I long for a drop of water.
sentences.inferno-30-s14: The little streams that descend from the green hills of the Casentino down into the Arno, making their channels cold and soft, are always before me, and not in vain; for the sight of them dries me ever more than the harm by which I strip the flesh from my face.
sentences.inferno-30-s15: Rigid Justice, which searches me, takes its cause from the place where I sinned, to put my sighs more to flight.
sentences.inferno-30-s16: There is Romena, there where I falsified the sealed league of the Baptist; for that reason I left my body burned up.
sentences.inferno-30-s33: 'And may the thirst that makes you burst be held guilty against you,' said the Greek; 'the tongue, and the rotten water that so crowds your belly before your eyes!'
sentences.inferno-30-s35: Then the moneyer said: "Thus your mouth is torn by your own evil, as is usual; for if I am thirsty and blood fills me, you have the burning thirst and the head that pains you, and to lick Narcissus' mirror you would not wish to summon many words."
sentences.inferno-30-s36: I was completely fixed on listening to them, when the master said to me: "Now then, look, for by a little I almost bristled with you!"
sentences.inferno-30-s5: And the Aretine who had stayed behind, trembling, said to me: "That little imp is Gianni Schicchi, and he goes about furious, treating others like this."
sentences.inferno-30-s6: "Oh," I said to him, "if the other doesn't sink his teeth into you, it won't be any trouble for you to tell who he is, before he breaks away from here."
sentences.inferno-30-s7: And he to me: "That is the ancient soul of wicked Myrrha, who became, toward her father and outside of rightful love, a lover."
sentences.inferno-30-s8: She thus came to sin with him, falsifying herself into another's form; as the other one who goes yonder asserted, in order to gain the woman of the troop, to falsify Buoso Donati in himself, making a will and giving the will its terms."
sentences.inferno-30-s41: And it is reasonable that I should always be at your side, if it should happen that Fortune receives you where there are people of a similar disposition; for to desire to hear that is a low desire.
sentences.inferno-30-s21: And I to him, "Who are the two poor wretches who give off steam like hands wet with winter, lying pressed against your right-hand borders?"
sentences.inferno-30-s23: "I found them here — and afterwards they did not turn back —," he answered, "when I fell into this slope, and I do not believe they will remain there for ever."
sentences.inferno-30-s24: One of them is the woman who falsely accused Joseph; the other is the false Sinon, a Greek of Troy: because of an acute fever they emit such a stench.
sentences.inferno-30-s17: But if I were to see here the sad soul of Guido or of Alessandro or of their brother, I would not give up the sight for Fonte Branda.
sentences.inferno-30-s18: One of them is already inside, if the enraged shades that go about say true; but what use is that to me — I have my limbs bound?
sentences.inferno-30-s19: If I were even still so light that I could in a hundred years move an inch, I would already be set on the path, searching for him among these wretched people, although it (she/it) encircles eleven miles, and it does not have even half a crosswise mile.
sentences.inferno-30-s20: I belong among them in such a company; they induced me to strike the florins which had three carats of dross.
sentences.inferno-30-s1: At the time when Juno was angry at Semele over the Theban bloodline, as each occasion showed, Athamas grew so insane that, seeing his wife with two children going, each held by one hand, he cried: "Let us stretch the nets, so that I may seize the she‑lion and the lion‑cubs at the pass"; and then he outstretched his pitiless claws, seizing the one who had the name Learchus, rolled him and dashed him against a rock; and she drowned herself with the other burden.
sentences.inferno-30-s2: And when Fortune turned low the loftiness of the Trojans who dared everything, so that together with the kingdom the king was overthrown, Hecuba — sad, wretched and miserable — after she saw Polyxena dead and became aware on the seashore of her Polydorus, the sorrowful woman howled madly like a dog; so much did grief distort her mind.
sentences.inferno-30-s3: But neither Theban furies nor Trojan women were ever seen so cruel; they do not sting beasts, much less human limbs, as I saw in two pale, naked shades who ran on biting in that way which the pig exhibits when it gets out of the sty.
sentences.inferno-30-s4: One of them reached Capocchio, and at the knot of his neck she gnawed him, so that, pulling, she made his belly scrape down to the very firm bottom.
sentences.inferno-30-s38: When I heard him speak to me in anger, I turned toward him with such shame that even now the memory turns me.
sentences.inferno-30-s39: As is the man who dreams his own ruin, who while dreaming longs to dream (again), so that what is, as if it were not, he yearns for — so did I make myself; unable to speak, I longed to excuse myself, and kept excusing myself, and he would not believe me able to do it.
sentences.inferno-30-s40: "A greater fault washes away a lesser shame," said the master, "for yours was not so; therefore I free you from every sorrow."
